run_5 Network-Exposure Analysis

This report validates whether the empirical exposure-network layer is functioning in run 5 and summarizes the first descriptive insights from the four-state measurement backbone: private baseline (B), baseline network exposure (BN), private post-attack (P), and post-attack network exposure (PN).

Profiles assigned
60
empirical positions
BN leaf tasks
1080
0 fallbacks
PN leaf tasks
1080
0 fallbacks
Scenario rows
1080
Stage 05 output
Main result. Run 5 supports the new exposure layer mechanically and analytically. The network phases use empirical incoming exposure edges, not profile similarity; the post-attack network phase adds a positive mean direction-aware increment of 8.60 score points after private attack exposure.

1. Measurement Backbone Is Complete

The first validation question is whether every intended phase exists at the correct unit of analysis. The assignment phase operates once per profile, BN once per profile-opinion task, PN once per profile-opinion-attack condition, and Stage 05 expands the completed measurements into the scenario-level analysis table.

phase expected_unit completed fallback_or_skipped core_validity_check
01b profile-position assignment unique profile 60 0 all profiles assigned to one empirical position
02b baseline network exposure profile × opinion 1080 0 same-opinion incoming empirical peers
04b post-attack network exposure profile × opinion leaf (attacks pooled) 1080 0 same-condition incoming empirical peers
05 effect construction scenario row 1080 0 B, BN, P, PN and exposure covariates flattened

2. Peer Contexts Are Empirical Exposure Neighborhoods

The central implementation claim is that peer context now follows empirical directed exposure edges: source_position_id -> target_position_id, meaning visible peer to exposed receiver. The context payloads contain exposure_weight and no affinity field. Full incoming peer metrics are preserved, while prompt rationales are bounded to at most eight exemplars.

3. Network Exposure Changes The Measurement State

The four-state design separates three quantities that should not be collapsed: the baseline peer-context shift (BN - B), the private attack effect ((P - B) × d), and the post-attack network increment ((PN - P) × d). In run 5, the average private attack effect is 16.83, the average post-network increment is 8.60, and the average final network-exposed effect is 25.43.

4. Private Susceptibility Differs By Separable Attack Factor

Each scenario carries its own near-unique DISARM Plan/Prepare/Execute triplet, so a single opinion x full-triplet cell is a singleton. Instead the triplet is decomposed into SEPARABLE attack factors (the shared 2nd-level tactic node of each phase), and each factor's marginal contribution to the direction-aware effect is estimated across the many triplets. This isolates the individual contribution of the attack factor rather than confounding it with one specific operation.

5. Exposure To Adversarially-Shifted Peers Drives Network Amplification

This is the most direct, receiver-level validation of the network layer, measured at the level the opinion is actually elicited. The driver is the direction-aware peer-position pull: how far the exposure-weighted incoming-peer consensus sits toward the attacker's goal relative to the profile's own private post-attack score. When that pull is positive (peers are more shifted toward the goal than the profile), the profile should amplify toward the goal; when it is negative (peers resist), the profile should dampen. Run 5 shows exactly this monotonic relationship (r = +0.24): the binned post-network increment rises smoothly with the peer-position pull and flips sign with it. This is the social-conformity mechanism that makes the exposure network propagate the attack, and it is the clean test of the hypothesis (the condition-level placement view in section 9 is additionally confounded by an amplification ceiling, so this receiver-level relationship is the primary network-propagation evidence).

relationship x y pearson_r n unit

6. Exposure Position Adds Sender And Receiver Structure

The assigned graph positions are not interchangeable. High-visibility senders carry much higher outgoing visibility, high-exposure receivers have the highest incoming exposure, and bridge/peripheral/context positions remain structurally distinct. In this 60-profile run, role-level outcome differences are descriptive rather than decisive, but the required covariates for H3 and H4 are present.

exposure_display_role n mean_private_attack_effect mean_post_network_increment mean_total_network_effect mean_incoming_exposure mean_outgoing_visibility mean_bridge_score

high_exposure_receiver 234 11.568376 7.341880 18.910256 29.742165 213.445206 0.307297

7. Candidate Vulnerability Hubs And Resilience Anchors

This section directly operationalizes the network subquestion: whether private susceptibility aligns with empirical exposure-network position. Private susceptibility is measured as mean_ae_private = mean((P - B) × d), while network position is measured as direct exposure sender reach exposure_outgoing_visibility_weight = sum_i w_(j->i). High susceptibility alone marks individual vulnerability; high susceptibility combined with high sender reach marks a candidate population-level vulnerability hub. High sender reach combined with low susceptibility marks a possible resilience anchor.

In run 5, susceptibility and sender reach are only weakly aligned at the profile level (r = -0.04). That makes this plot useful for identifying individual high-leverage profiles, but it should not be read as final H3/H4 evidence.

8. Primary Alignment Metric

The primary scenario-level network metric is reach_weighted_susceptibility - unweighted_susceptibility. It asks whether the empirical sender-reach mass (outgoing visibility) is shifted toward profiles with higher or lower private attack susceptibility in each opinion domain × Execute tactic condition. The z-standardized version is used below so conditions with different susceptibility scales are comparable.

In run 5, the strongest centrality shift toward susceptible profiles occurs for Information Integrity And Platforms × Maximise Exposure (z = +0.44). The strongest shift toward resilient profiles occurs for Information Integrity And Platforms × Persist in the Information Environment (z = -0.37).

9. Alignment Versus Network Attack Effect (Ecological View, Ceiling-Confounded)

Read section 5 first. The clean test of the network hypothesis is the receiver-level peer-position pull in section 5 (r = +0.24, monotonic), because it is measured at the level the opinion is elicited. The condition-level alignment below is a coarser ecological view and is confounded by an amplification ceiling: conditions whose profiles are already strongly shifted by the individual attack have little remaining headroom for the network to add, so concentrating susceptibility on high-reach profiles can coincide with a smaller mean increment even though the underlying propagation is positive. The condition-level correlations therefore should not be read as the network test; they are reported for completeness and transparency.

The primary condition-level outcome is the network-specific increment mean_pn_increment_effectivity = mean((PN - P) × d). The final endpoint mean_ae_total_network = mean((PN - B) × d) is reported for completeness, but it is dominated by the individual layer (ae_private), so its association with the alignment partly reflects a within-condition deviation-vs-level relationship rather than a network effect.

The strictest, conformity-adjusted definition is mean_net_social_amplification_effectivity = mean(((PN - P) - (BN - B)) × d): a difference-in-differences that subtracts the generic baseline peer-conformity pull (BN - B) from the post-attack peer pull (PN - P), isolating the part of the network shift that is specifically attack-driven rather than ordinary social conformity.

The hypothesis-consistent pattern is therefore: positive centrality-susceptibility alignment should correspond to higher mean_ae_total_network and, more directly, higher mean_pn_increment_effectivity and mean_net_social_amplification_effectivity; negative alignment should correspond to lower values. Because the within-condition alignment is only estimable when a condition holds enough profiles, the associations are reported over the 6 of 6 conditions that meet the 8-profile floor. In run 5 these are r = +0.17 for total network-exposed effect, r = +0.05 for the post-network increment and r = +0.36 for the conformity-adjusted amplification.

10. Condition-Specific Vulnerability Planes

The averaged hub analysis can hide condition-specific structure. The sharper diagnostic is therefore one centrality-by-susceptibility map for each opinion domain × Execute tactic configuration. Within each panel the profiles carry their empirical outgoing visibility (sender reach) and the private susceptibility AE_private realised under that exact opinion domain and Execute tactic. The small label in each panel reports the primary alignment metric as centrality shift z.

11. Interpretation And Limits Before Sharing

This run validates the empirical exposure-network layer end to end on the integrated production design: the empirical graph is used, the BN and PN cluster phases complete for every profile, and the resulting quantities answer the intended questions. It is a pilot (60 profiles, one sampled integrated scenario panel) and should not yet be read as final inferential evidence.

Pipeline note. BN and PN are elicited cluster-at-once over the opinion parent cluster and joined back to the per-leaf long table; the mean absolute baseline network increment |BN - B| is 51.76. Each scenario carries its own near-unique DISARM Plan/Prepare/Execute triplet, so attack effects are estimated as separable phase-tactic factor contributions rather than per-triplet cells.

Source run: run_5. Graph: politisky24_bluesky_v1. Generated from completed pipeline artifacts only; no new LLM calls were made.
